content.subtitle: Form
items: if + present simple, present simple

content.subtitle: Use
items: General or scientific facts and definitions: If you have faith in something, you believe in something you cannot prove.

content.subtitle: Form
items: if + a present tense, will + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Real or likely conditions in the present or future and their results: If you have a birthday party, you'll get loads of cool presents!

content.subtitle: Variations
items: We can also use may, might, can, could, shall, should, ought to, have to instead of will: If you have a birthday party, you might get loads of cool presents!
We can also use should + bare infinitive instead of present simple (suggests the situation is possible, but unlikely): If you should bump into Alex at the concert, you'll be able to get a lift home.
We can also use a form of the first conditional to give instructions: if + a present tense, imperative: If you decide to have a birthday party, tell me!

sections.title: Unless, in case, as/so long as, provided (that)

items: unless: 'except if' or 'if…not': I'll be there at six unless I get delayed.
in case: 'because he/she/it/etc might': Let's take our wellies in case it's muddy.
as/so long as: 'if' or 'only if': As long as I'm happy, my parents don't care what job I do.
provided (that): 'if' or 'only if': Provided (that) I'm happy, my parents don't care what job I do.

content.subtitle: Form
items: if + past simple or past continuous, would + bare infinitive

content.subtitle: Use
items: Impossible, unlikely or hypothetical conditions in the present or future and their results: If you had a beard, you would look just like Charles Dickens!
Advice: If I were you, I would think very carefully about my future.

content.subtitle: Variations
items: We can also use might or could instead of would: If we were older, we could go on holiday on our own.
We can also use could in the if clause (was/were able to): If I could drive, I'd buy a car.

content.subtitle: Form
items: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
items: Hypothetical conditions in the past and their results in the past: If you had worn a fake beard, no one would have known who you were!

content.subtitle: Variations
items: We can also use might, could or should instead of would: If I had done some revision, I might / could / should have passed the exam.

content.subtitle: Type 1
items: Form: if + past perfect (simple or continuous), would + bare infinitive
Use: Hypothetical past condition and a present result: If I had listened to my parents, I wouldn't be in so much trouble now.

content.subtitle: Type 2
items: Form: if + past simple or past continuous, would + have + past participle
Use: Hypothetical present condition and a past result: If I had a mobile, I would have called you last night.

content.subtitle: Form
items: Should I/you/he/etc... instead of If I/you/he/etc should...
Were I/you/he/etc... instead of If I/you/he/etc were...
Had I/you/he/etc... instead of If I/you/he/etc had...

content.subtitle: Use
items: More formal form of the first conditional (with should): Should the situation worsen, the United Nations is prepared to send in a peacekeeping force.
More formal form of the second conditional: Were the situation to worsen, the United Nations would be prepared to send in a peacekeeping force.
More formal form of the third conditional: Had the situation worsened, the United Nations would have been prepared to send in a peacekeeping force.

For all conditional sentences, when the condition comes before the result it is usually followed by a comma. When the result comes first, no comma is necessary: I would have called you last night if I had a mobile.
In conditional sentences, modals (will, would, could, etc) are sometimes followed by a continuous infinitive: We'd still be waiting if you hadn't turned up.

American English note: In informal conversation, US speakers sometimes use would or would have in the if clause. UK: How would you feel if this happened to you? US: How would you feel if this would happen to you?

sections.title: A: Understanding conditionals
sections.instructions: Answer the questions about each sentence.

items.sentence: If Carl doesn't come to the party, I'll be really upset.
items.answer: Is it possible that Carl won't come to the party? YES

items.sentence: If my neighbour became prime minister, he'd give everyone a million euros.
items.answer: Is it likely that the neighbour will become prime minister? NO. Is it possible? YES

items.sentence: If Helen weren't here, I don't know what we'd do.
If Tarek had had enough money, he'd have bought a car.
items.answer: Did Tarek have enough money? NO. Did he buy a car? NO

items.sentence: If Debbie hadn't gone to the UK, she wouldn't have met Will.
items.answer: Did Debbie go to the UK? YES. Did she meet Will? YES

items.sentence: If the police had investigated the case thoroughly, they wouldn't have arrested Keren.
items.answer: Did the police investigate the case thoroughly? NO. Did they arrest Keren? YES

items.sentence: Had she not gone to university, she might not have started her own business.
items.answer: Did she go to university? YES. Did she start her own business? YES

sections.title: B: Complete the definitions
sections.instructions: Complete the definitions using your own ideas.

items.sentence: If you bully someone,
items.answer: you don't treat them with respect.

items.sentence: If you accuse someone,
items.answer: you say they have done something wrong.

items.sentence: If you are a liar,
If you are rich,
If you are lucky,
If you are lazy,
If you look up to someone,
If you look down on someone,

sections.title: C: First conditional
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box.

items.sentence: If she ...... at five o'clock, she'll be there by half past seven.
We ...... you if we have any problems.
It might not be a good idea to go out tonight if you ...... an important test in the morning.
If you ...... ill all day, you shouldn't come to the club tonight.
If you should ...... my wallet, call me on my mobile immediately!
Let's get a different DVD if you ...... that one already.
If Sean ...... so hard lately, he'll welcome the chance to have a few days off.
If you're going into town, ...... a video for tonight while you're there!
If you see Carol tonight, ...... to say hello from me!
Don't feel you have to come if you ...... to.

sections.title: D: Unless, in case, as long as, provided
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: This time in six months I'll be at university **unless / if** I decide to take a year off first.
We'd better leave early tomorrow **unless / in case** there's a lot of traffic when we get to Doncaster.
You can watch the film **as long as / in case** you promise to go straight to bed when it finishes.
Let's go to Mirabella's tonight **unless / if** you haven't been there before.
**Unless / So long as** I've still got my health, I don't mind how poor I am!
You'd better take a sweater with you now **if / in case** it gets cold tonight.
Do what you like **provided / unless** you don't make any noise.
I'm not going to worry **unless / as long as** she hasn't called by midnight.

sections.title: E: Second conditional
sections.instructions: Write sentences using the second conditional.

items.sentence: I don't live alone so I don't get lonely.
items.answer: If I lived alone, I'd get lonely.

items.sentence: Don's got such a lot of homework that he's not going to play football tonight.
items.answer: If Don didn't have so much homework, he'd play football tonight.

items.sentence: I can't swim so I'm not going scuba diving with Terry.
items.answer: If I could swim, I'd go scuba diving with Terry.

items.sentence: We're not going to order a pizza because we don't have enough money.
items.answer: We'd order pizza if we had enough money.

items.sentence: We're not staying in the same hotel, so we can't share a room.
items.answer: If we were staying in the same hotel, we could share a room.

items.sentence: I'm not you, but I think you should call Antony right now!
items.answer: If I were you, I'd call Antony right now.

items.sentence: I can't come because I have to help my dad with something.
items.answer: I'd come if I didn't have to help my dad with something.

items.sentence: I don't go to bed as late as you so I'm able to get up early in the morning.
items.answer: If I went to bed as late as you, I wouldn't be able to get up early in the morning.

sections.title: F: Third conditional
sections.instructions: Read the text and complete the sentences using the third conditional.

items.sentence: If I'd started learning English a year earlier,
items.answer: I would have done this book last year!

items.sentence: If a meteor hadn't struck the Earth,
items.answer: the dinosaurs wouldn't have become extinct.

items.sentence: If my best friend had won the lottery last summer,
items.answer: he/she would have moved to a bigger house.

items.sentence: If my parents hadn't met,
Everyone on board the Titanic would have survived
items.answer: if there had been enough lifeboats.

items.sentence: Van Gogh wouldn't have died in poverty
items.answer: if he had been recognised as a great painter during his lifetime.

items.sentence: Madonna wouldn't have made any records
If George Bush hadn't become President of the USA in January 2001,
items.answer: lots of things would have been different.

items.sentence: If Greece hadn't won the European Cup in July 2004,
items.answer: I would have been very upset.

items.sentence: If this theory about parallel universes (1) ...... correct, it (2) ...... that we're living in more than one universe at the same time.
it (2) ...... that we're living in more than one universe at the same time.
But if that (3) ...... true - if I was doing different things in different universes - (4) ...... about it.
if I was doing different things in different universes - (4) ...... about it.
And if I (5) ...... about it, how could it be me doing these things?
If I (6) ...... the lottery last year in a parallel universe, and decided to sail round the world, how is that 'me'?
If I do something, it (7) ...... consequences.
For example, if I (8) ...... study, I might not pass my exams.
If I just (9) ...... about the universe I actually live in, then I'm sure (10) ...... okay!
then I'm sure (10) ...... okay!

sections.title: H: Mixed conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: If (you / do) your homework last night, you'd know the answer to this question!
We (not / stand) by the side of the road at the moment trying to get a lift if (we / bring) a spare tyre with us.
If I (not / stay up) so late last night, I (not / feel) so tired this morning!
items.answer: hadn't stayed up, wouldn't be feeling

items.sentence: (I / be) in terrible trouble right now if you (not / help) me.
If I had a video recorder, (I / record) the match last night.
(I / ask) for Andy's phone number when I met him if I (not / already / have) a boyfriend!
items.answer: I'd have asked, didn't already have

items.sentence: If Cody (not / live) so far away, he (not / be) so late to the barbecue yesterday.
Tracy (not / need) extra lessons last month if she (be) as good at maths as you are.
(you / get) a job when you left school if the university (not / offer) you a place?
items.answer: Would you have got, hadn't offered

items.sentence: If you (have) as much money as she does, (you / retire) by now?

sections.title: I: Inverted conditionals
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence:
If her condition should improve, we'll inform you immediately. (Should)
Should ......, we'll inform you immediately.

Jade will ask lots of difficult questions if she gets to interview a famous politician. (Should)
Should ......, she will ask lots of difficult questions.
items.answer: Jade get to interview a famous politician

items.sentence:
If you were able to go abroad for the summer, where would you go? (Were)
Were ......, where would you go?
items.answer: you able to go abroad for the summer

items.sentence:
If I was to become a vet, I'd find putting animals down very difficult. (Were)
Were ......, I'd find putting animals down very difficult.

The world would be much better off if all environmental pollution stopped today. (Were)
Were ......, the world would be much better off.
items.answer: all environmental pollution to stop today

items.sentence:
If the hole in the ozone layer had been discovered sooner, fewer people would have got skin cancer. (Had)
Had ......, fewer people would have got skin cancer.
items.answer: the hole in the ozone layer been discovered sooner

items.sentence:
I wouldn't have become a teacher if I hadn't had such a good English teacher at school. (Had)
Had ......, I wouldn't have become a teacher.
items.answer: I not had such a good English teacher at school

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: If a line is correct, put a tick (✓). If there is an extra word in a line, write it next to the number.

items.sentence: I'm an only child. People often say to me: 'Wouldn't you be a lot
happier if you have had brothers and sisters?' but I don't see it like that.
It's true that if I had had a brother or sister, I would have someone closer
to my own age to talk to and play with at home, but I don't think that's
very important provided for you have close friends, which I do.
If my parents would had more children, they wouldn't be able to spend so much
time with me. And we have great fun together! Also, except if I had a
brother or sister, I'd have it to share a bedroom with them.
That might be fun, but what would happen if I wanted to play my CDs and he or she had
to study? No - I don't want a brother or sister, unless it will happens, of course. In that case, I'll think it's the best thing in the world!
